era_context: The early 16th century saw the Portuguese maritime empire expanding rapidly under Manuel I, the Ottoman Empire under Bayezid II in its final years, the Ming Dynasty in China under the Zhengde Emperor, and the Mughal Empire emerging under Babur. The Italian Renaissance was at its height with major artistic achievements. In the Americas, the Aztec and Inca empires remained at their territorial peaks. This was a period of significant global transformation, with European expansion beginning to reshape trade networks and geopolitical relationships.

events.title: Battle of Diu (Portuguese naval victory against Mamluk-Bijapur alliance)
events.description: Portuguese naval forces under Afonso de Albuquerque defeated a combined fleet of the Mamluk Sultanate (Egypt) and the Bijapur Sultanate in the waters off Diu. This naval victory was crucial in establishing Portuguese dominance in the Indian Ocean. The battle demonstrated the superiority of Portuguese naval technology, particularly cannon-armed ships, over traditional galley-based fleets. The victory secured Portuguese control over key trade routes and prevented organized Arab and Egyptian resistance to Portuguese expansion in the Indian Ocean.
events.certainty_note: The battle is well-documented in Portuguese sources. Dating varies between late 1509 and early 1510 in different sources; May 1509 is sometimes cited.

events.title: Henry VIII becomes King of England
events.description: Henry VIII ascended to the English throne following the death of his father Henry VII on April 21, 1509. The young king, aged 17, inherited a stable and prosperous kingdom. Henry VIII's accession marked the beginning of one of the most consequential reigns in English history. His early years were characterized by relative peace and cultural flourishing, though his later reign would be marked by religious upheaval, military campaigns, and dynastic turmoil. His marriage to Catherine of Aragon in June 1509 was celebrated as a diplomatic triumph.
events.certainty_note: Henry VIII's accession date is firmly established as April 21, 1509. His marriage to Catherine of Aragon occurred on June 11, 1509.

events.title: Earthquake in Constantinople (Istanbul)
events.description: A major earthquake struck Constantinople on September 14, 1509, causing significant damage to buildings and infrastructure. The earthquake killed thousands of people and caused widespread destruction. Contemporary accounts describe the devastation and the subsequent rebuilding efforts. The earthquake is sometimes called the 'Earthquake of the Forty Days' due to the duration of aftershocks. The disaster had economic and social consequences for the Ottoman capital, though the city recovered relatively quickly.
events.certainty_note: The earthquake is well-documented in Ottoman and Byzantine sources. The date of September 14, 1509 is firmly established.

events.title: Zhengde Emperor's reign in Ming China
events.description: The Zhengde Emperor continued his reign, characterized by increasing eunuch influence and the emperor's personal indulgence. The eunuch Liu Jin wielded enormous power over state affairs. Despite governance issues, the Ming Dynasty maintained economic prosperity and cultural vitality. The period saw continued artistic and literary achievements, though political stability was compromised by the emperor's disinterest in traditional governance.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Zhengde Emperor's reign and eunuch influence are well-documented in Ming records.

events.title: Babur's military campaigns in Central Asia
events.description: Babur, the future founder of the Mughal Empire, was engaged in military campaigns to expand his territory in Central Asia. He was consolidating power and eliminating rival sultanates. Babur's military innovations, including the use of gunpowder artillery and cavalry tactics, were beginning to reshape warfare in the region. His campaigns in this period laid the groundwork for his eventual conquest of northern India.
events.certainty_note: Babur's campaigns in this period are documented in his autobiography, though specific events in 1509 are less precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Diu is sometimes dated to February 1510 rather than 1509; sources vary on the exact date. Some modern scholarship questions whether the battle was as decisive as Portuguese sources claim, noting that Arab and Indian merchant networks continued to operate alongside Portuguese control. The significance of Henry VIII's accession is sometimes overstated in English historiography; his early reign was relatively peaceful and his major religious and political upheavals came later.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1509 is rich, particularly for Portuguese, English, Ottoman, and Chinese sources. Portuguese colonial records are detailed. English royal records are comprehensive. Ottoman and Chinese sources are abundant. However, sources from the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia are less abundant in European archives. The perspective is heavily weighted toward European, Ottoman, and Chinese court records. Indigenous perspectives from conquered regions are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: Portuguese naval victory at Diu enabled the capture of Goa in 1510
Henry VIII's accession eventually led to the Break with Rome in 1534
